primary_diagnosis.name: Infantile seborrhoeic dermatitis (cradle cap) with intertriginous weeping

pathophysiology_summary: Infantile seborrhoeic dermatitis (cradle cap) is a benign, self-limiting response to commensal Malassezia yeast in sebaceous-rich sites, producing greasy yellow scale/crust on the scalp with poorly-marginated erythematous plaques on the forehead, eyebrows, nasolabial folds and flexures (post-auricular, axillae). Here friction and parental application of occlusive oils (which feed the yeast) have caused maceration, serous weeping and a small fissure with pinpoint bleeding behind the ears. It is typically non-pruritic-to-mildly-itchy and the infant is systemically well. Discriminators: greasy yellow scale with early flexural/post-auricular involvement and sparing of the extensor cheeks distinguishes it from atopic (infantile) eczema; serous intertriginous weeping without thick honey-crust distinguishes it from impetigo; and the absence of petechiae/purpura, failure to thrive or systemic illness argues against Langerhans cell histiocytosis (a rare but important mimic). Management is stopping the occlusive oils, a MILD topical hydrocortisone/antifungal (clotrimazole) combination for a short course to the weeping/inflamed areas, gentle mechanical de-crusting of the scalp with baby shampoo and a soft brush, and parental reassurance, with a safety-net for secondary bacterial infection and systemic illness.

differential.evidence_basis: Greasy yellow scalp crust + seborrhoeic facial plaques + post-auricular weeping, oil-aggravated.
Weeping fissured skin can superinfect — screen for honey crust/spread/fever.
Dry itchy extensor/cheek involvement, not greasy yellow scale with flexural onset.
A rare mimic — screen for petechiae/purpura, systemic illness, failure to thrive.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Diagnose cradle cap, stop occlusive oils, use only mild short-course topical therapy, and screen for secondary infection and serious mimics (LCH).

differential.evidence_basis: Classic greasy scale; systemically well, no secondary infection or petechiae/purpura.
Not established now; remains a safety-net given the weeping fissure.
Working diagnosis: stop oils, mild hydrocortisone/clotrimazole short course, gentle scalp care, reassurance; infection/systemic safety-net.

red_flags.description: Secondary bacterial infection (honey crust, spreading hot redness, pus) or unwell infant (fever, poor feeding, lethargy)
red_flags.clinical_significance: Weeping fissured skin can superinfect; a febrile/poorly-feeding young infant needs urgent assessment.

red_flags.description: Petechiae/purpura, failure to thrive or systemic illness (possible Langerhans cell histiocytosis / other serious disease)
red_flags.clinical_significance: Rare but serious mimics of severe/refractory seborrhoeic dermatitis.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Not stopping the oils that feed the yeast and worsen the flare.
Using potent/long-course steroids on infant skin.
Not screening for secondary infection or LCH/systemic illness.

telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Stop occlusive oils, prescribe a mild hydrocortisone/antifungal combination short course to weeping/inflamed areas, gentle scalp de-crusting, and reassure; paediatric ED if secondary infection, fever/poor feeding, or petechiae/purpura/systemic illness.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Continuing oils, over-treating, or missing secondary infection/serious systemic disease.

guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: Infantile seborrhoeic dermatitis: gentle scalp care and emollients; short course of a mild topical corticosteroid ± antifungal for inflamed areas; it is benign and self-limiting; avoid occlusive irritants.
